coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with 3-aminoquinoline and citric acid as an absorbent in full ultraviolet bands and application of transparent sunscreen umbrella
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yulin
Xu, Zhiqiang
Chen, Congcong
Ye, Weihao
Guo, Baoyan
Qiu, Jiemin
Zhuang, Jianle
Hu, Chaofan
Lei, Bingfu
Hu, Guangqi
Liu, Yingliang
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, as the global climate continues to deteriorate, the harm of ultraviolet(UV) radiation to the human body is increasing, but the development of commercial UV-blocking products has not followed the corresponding update. Carbon dots (CDs) are excellent candidates for UV absorbers because of their multi-functional groups, high stability, and low toxicity. In this study, full UV bond absorbing CDs (3A-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using anhydrous Citric acid(CA) and 3-aminoquinoline as raw materials. The 3A-CDs have super high UV absorption efficiency, light-aging resistance, and acid and alkaline resistance. Subsequently, different concentrations of 3A-CDs were doped into PVA to prepare 3A-CDs@PVA films with excellent full UV bond absorption and good mechanical properties. Finally, 3A-CDs@PVA films were sandwiched between two pieces of transparent umbrella cloth. The 0.3 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film with transparent umbrella cloth can achieve a blocking efficiency of more than 97.2% in the full UV bands. Also, SPF is greater than 50, PFA is PA++++, and the visible light transmittance is as high as 93.8%. This study provides a new application and prospect for the development of CDs and their composite products into new, efficient, and durable UV-blocking products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nitrogen-doped
Full UV bonds absorption
High transmission in the visible

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of 3A-CDs@PVA films with transparent umbrella cloth
UV blocking effect of the new transparent sunscreen umbrella

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) TEM and HRTEM images of 3A-CDs. (c) Roman spectra of 3A-CDs. (d and e) XRD and FT-IR spectra of 3A-CDs. (f–i) XPS survey, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra.

(a) UV absorption spectra and transmittance spectra of 3A-CDs and photograph (inset) under visible light and a 365 nm UV lamp. (b–f) Absorption spectra of 3A-CDs with different precursor proportions, pH values in solution, reaction temperatures, reaction times, and various concentrations. (g) Schematic diagram of the synthesis mechanism of 3A-CDs and its corresponding application.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PLE, PL spectra of 3A-CDs and picture of 3A-CDs solution under visible and UV light (inset). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of 3A-CDs. (c and d) PLE and PL spectra of 3A-CDs with different pH values in solution.

(a) Pictures of 3A-CDs in solution before UV radiation. (b and c) UV–Vis absorption and transmission curves of 3A-CDs at different aging times. (d) FT-IR spectra of 3A-CDs with different pH values in solution. (e) Acid and alkaline resistance of fluorescence and UV–Vis transmission of 3A-CDs.
(a) Image of the transparency and (b) UV blocking ability of the 3A-CDs@PVA films. (c) Images of mixed 3A-CDs@PVA films containing various amounts of 3A-CDs.

(a) Schematic diagram of the UV irradiation meter usage. (b) Variation of intensity and blocking efficiency under daylight and (c) UV-light with increasing content of 3A-CDs. (d) Intensity and blocking efficiency of 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA films at different aging times.

(a) UV–Vis transmission curve of 3A-CDs@PVA films doped with different concentrations of 3A-CDs. (b) UV–Vis transmission spectra of 0.4 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA at different aging times. (c) Tensile mechanical properties and (d) FT-IR spectra of 3A-CDs@PVA films doped with different concentrations of 3A-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis transmission curve of transparent umbrella cloth, 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA films, and 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA films with transparent umbrella cloth. (b) Variation of intensity and blocking efficiency under daylight with increasing content of 3A-CDs. (c) The application diagram of the new transparent sunscreen umbrella.
Analysis of the blocking rate of UV, UVA and UVB and the corresponding SPF, PFA and visible transmittance of 3A-CDs@PVA films doped with different concentrations of 3A-CDs with umbrella cloth.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots with 3-aminoquinoline and citric acid as an absorbent in full ultraviolet bands and application of transparent sunscreen umbrella

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Biomassed Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Centre for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biomassed Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Centre for Optical Agriculture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Biomassed Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Centre for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou, 510642, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou, 510665, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Photoelectric Engineering, Guangdong Polytechnic Normal University, Guangzhou 510665, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, as the global climate continues to deteriorate, the harm of ultraviolet(UV) radiation to the human body is increasing, but the development of commercial UV-blocking products has not followed the corresponding update. Carbon dots (CDs) are excellent candidates for UV absorbers because of their multi-functional groups, high stability, and low toxicity. In this study, full UV bond absorbing CDs (3A-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using anhydrous Citric acid(CA) and 3-aminoquinoline as raw materials. The 3A-CDs have super high UV absorption efficiency, light-aging resistance, and acid and alkaline resistance. Subsequently, different concentrations of 3A-CDs were doped into PVA to prepare 3A-CDs@PVA films with excellent full UV bond absorption and good mechanical properties. Finally, 3A-CDs@PVA films were sandwiched between two pieces of transparent umbrella cloth. The 0.3 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film with transparent umbrella cloth can achieve a blocking efficiency of more than 97.2% in the full UV bands. Also, SPF is greater than 50, PFA is PA++++, and the visible light transmittance is as high as 93.8%. This study provides a new application and prospect for the development of CDs and their composite products into new, efficient, and durable UV-blocking products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the global climate change in recent decades, the ozone layer has experienced long-term depletion, its composition continues to change, its thickness becomes thinner, and the UV radiation dose increases accordingly. According to the different wavelengths, UV light can be divided into three parts: UVC (200–280 nm), UVB (280–320 nm), and UVA (320–400 nm) [1]. Among them, the UVC will be absorbed by the ozone layer and can't reach the surface of the earth. The intensity of UVB and UVA radiation is greatly lower than visible light and infrared radiation, but its photon energy exceeds the energy of C–C. When UV radiation is combined with oxygen in the air, it can produce free radicals in polymer materials, eventually leading to the degradation of polymer materials [2]. Prolonged exposure of human skin to sunlight without protection can also lead to a variety of harmful effects. Among them, UVB, as the main active band on earth, can cause chronic effects such as erythema, skin burns, skin photoaging and immunosuppression [3], or direct photochemical damage to human DNA and cause gene mutations, which is one of the main factors in the development of skin cancer [4]. UVA can induce skin tanning [5] and eye damage [6]. Long-term exposure can cause skin damage, make the skin lose elasticity, cause wrinkles to appear, and induce phototoxic and photoallergic reactions.

Combined with the increasing incidence of skin cancer reported worldwide in recent years [7], the public demand for various types of sunscreen products, such as sunglasses [8], umbrellas, clothing with a sunscreen function, and cosmetics with a sunscreen formula has considerably increased. However, we found that today's parasols are made of non-transparent fabric coated with a black UV layer, which is not only unsightly but also dangerous because it blocks people's view when necessary. As a result, there is a growing demand for solid transparent materials with high UV blocking efficiency that can be manufactured by adding appropriate UV absorbers to a transparent polymer matrix, such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [9], poly methyl methacrylate (PMMA) [10], etc [11,12]. Although organic UV absorbers (such as salicylic acid, benzophenone, benzotriazole, triazine, etc.) and inorganic UV absorbers (such as TiO2, ZnO, etc.) can be used as UV-blocking materials [8], their transparency is low, and they are unable to complete the UV blocking of the full UV bands. Also, their stability, acid and alkaline resistance, and light-aging resistance are not enough for long-term use. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new UV-blocking materials with elevated stability, high transparency, and full UV bond blocking performance.

CDs are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with particle sizes less than 10 nm [13]. They have the advantages of coordinated luminescence [14], optical and chemical stability [15], simple preparation [16], photobleaching [17] and acid/alkaline resistance [18], excellent biocompatibility [19] and environmental friendliness [20], and low toxicity [21]. This has promoted their wide applications in the following fields: light emitting diodes (LED) [22], photocatalysis [7], energy conversion/storage [23], biosensing, and a wide range of potential applications in numerous fields [24–26]. Since CDs have size limiting effects and photoluminescence properties [27], they can emit visible bonds under UV light, so they have excellent UV absorption properties [28]. However, there have been few studies on the use of CDs as UV absorbers in recent years. Xu et al. have prepared N-doping CDs from waste acorn cups and explored a UV absorber in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film [29]. However, as far as the UV absorption, these CDs can't block full UV bands as a UV absorber, and the transmission of visible light is low. Xie et al. prepared silane-doping GQDs (SiGQDs) which have full UV bond blocking and emit visible luminescence, but they are very inefficient at UV blocking [30]. Samuel C. Hess et al. obtained a transparent UV protection film which was synthesized from CQDs in an aqueous polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution. However, the visible bond transparency of the films is only 80% and they can only absorb the UVA band [9]. Chen et al. synthesized a full-band CDs UV absorber by expanding the CDs π-conjugation domain and capsulated CDs in a polyurethane (PU) matrix, but CDs@PU has a low transmittance at visible bond [2]. Therefore, we have focused on synthesising a kind of CDs as a UV absorber that can achieve full UV bond absorption with excellent absorption efficiency, aging resistance, and acid and alkali resistance.

In this paper, 3A-CDs was prepared by simple hydrothermal carbonization using anhydrous Citric acid(CA) and 3-aminoquinoline. There are strong absorption bands near 265 and 365 nm and a weak absorption near 320 nm. In terms of light-aging resistance and acid and alkali resistance showed that its UV absorption intensity remained above 97% and 99%, proved the excellent stability of 3A-CDs. Then, 3A-CDs@PVA films were synthesized and evaluated. It was found that 3A-CDs not only did not affect the original properties of PVA films but also endowed them with excellent full UV bond absorption performance, optical performance, and mechanical performance. Finally, the 3A-CDs@PVA films were sandwiched between two pieces of transparent umbrella cloth. The 0.3 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film with transparent umbrella cloth can achieve blocking efficiency of more than 97.2% in the full UV band, the SPF is greater than 50, PFA is PA++++, and the visible light transmittance is as high as 93.8%, meanwhile, reducing the cost and diluting the color of the film. At present, the synthetic process of 3A-CDs is simple, low-cost and environmentally friendly, and efficient. Stable full UV absorption performance has given it the potential to become a new generation of UV absorbers.

3-aminoquinoline (AR), anhydrous citric acid (AR), rhodamine B (AR), anhydrous ethanol (AR), and concentrated sulfuric acid (98.08%) were purchased from Aladdin Industries, Inc. Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) (AR) was purchased from Rouse Industries, Inc. All reagents were used without further purification. Ultrapure water was used in the whole experiment.

UV–Vis absorption and transmission curves were recorded by a UV–vis spectrofluorometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu). The visible luminescent curve was recorded by a fluorescence spectrofluorometer (F-7000, Hitachi). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by an SA-Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+ spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) graphics were recorded by a JEOL-2010 electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern tests of CDs were recorded by an Ultima IV (Rigaku). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded by a Nicolet IS10360 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher). The UV lamp used in all experiments gives 365 nm light and 85 W; it is produced by the Zoomlion UV Optical Factory. The Raman spectrum was recorded by an Alpha300R (Germany, Wetzlar). The UV intensities in the application of 3A-CDs@PVA film with a transparent umbrella cloth were recorded by a UV irradiometer (LS125, Linshang).

First, 0.01 M CA and 0.05 M 3-aminoquinoline were dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water with ultrasound for 10 min until they dissolved completely. Next, it was transferred into a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave equipped with PTFE liner and placed in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. When the solution was transformed from colourless into yellow, it indicated the successful synthesis of 3A-CDs. The 3A-CDs solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove large particles. Then, a dialysis bag with molecular weight of 500 Da was used to dialyze for 36 h to remove incomplete raw materials and intermediates. Finally, the purified product was suspended in an oven at 80 °C for 72 h to obtain yellow solid CDs and calculate the yield.

Under the condition of continuous stirring at 90 °C, 0.7 g of polyvinyl alcohol granules and the pre-prepared 3A-CDs solution were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. Then, they were poured into a plastic mould after being mixed well, and they were dried in the oven at 80 °C until the 3A-CDs@PVA films came off. The films with different 3A-CDs content (0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.3 wt%, 0.4 wt%, 0.5 wt%) were produced and stored in a dry container to protect the films from moisture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of 3A-CDs@PVA films with transparent umbrella cloth
ce:section.ce:para: The transparent umbrella cloth was cut to appropriate specifications and placed on the top and bottom of the 3A-CDs@PVA films, respectively. The superimposed products were placed in a sheet press with a force of 40 kN for 10 min, so as to obtain a smooth and close combination of multi-layer transparent UV-blocking umbrella cloth.

The TEM photographs in Fig. 1 a confirmed that 3A-CDs has excellent dispersion in an aqueous solution with an average particle size of about 2.7 nm. The HRTEM images in Fig. 1b showed 0.21 nm lattice stripes, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite [31]. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 1c shows two characteristic absorption bands at 1380 cm−1 (D-band) and 1573 cm−1 (G-band), respectively. The characteristic absorption band at 1380 cm−1 (D-band) indicates the existence of C–C of sp3 character, and the characteristic absorption band at 1573 cm−1 (G-band) indicates the existence of CC of sp2 character, where ID/IG = 1.03, suggesting a high degree of carbonization [32]. The XRD pattern of the 3A-CDs is given in Fig. 1d. It has the characteristic peak at 2θ = 22° corresponding to the graphite (100) plane. The result shows that 3A-CDs has a certain degree of carbonization, which is consistent with Raman and TEM results.

The chemical composition and structure of 3A-CDs were further investigated by FT-IR and XPS. In Fig. 1e, there is a broad absorption band at 3450 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of the N–H and –OH bonds [33]. The absorption peaks at 1652 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively [34]. According to the above discussion, the surface of 3A-CDs contains abundant functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2. The XPS full-scan spectra of the 3A-CDs and the high-resolution spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s are listed in Fig. 1f-i. The XPS full-scan spectra show that 3A-CDs is mainly composed of C, N, and O elements with binding energies of 285.08 eV, 400.08 eV, and 532.08 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively, see Fig. 1f, [35]. The percentages of C, N, and O atoms are 75.61%, 7.55%, and 16.84%, respectively. The C1s high-resolution spectrum in Fig. 1g shows the presence of C–H (283.56 eV), CC/C–C (284.63 eV), C–N (285.46 eV), C–O (286.32 eV), and CO/CN (288.61 eV) on the surface of 3A-CDs [36]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s could be fitted with three peaks at 398.81 eV, 399.48 eV, and 401.56 eV (Fig. 1h), which are attributed to N–H, pyridinic N, and graphitic N, respectively [37]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s can be fitted with two peaks at 531.80 eV and 533.48 eV (Fig. 1i), which are attributed to CO and C–O, respectively [38]. The results of XPS and FT-IR indicate that 3A-CDs contains –OH, –NH2, CO, CN, and other functional groups on the surface.

3A-CDs has excellent absorption in the full UV bands. According to Fig. 2 a, the 1.5 μg/mL 3A-CDs can achieve full UV bond absorption, and the average blocking efficiency is 98.8%. The absorption peak at 260–270 nm is generally attributed to the π-π* leap of the CC bond, which originates from the conjugated π orbital of the carbon ring [39]. The weak absorption peak at 320 nm and the strong absorption peak at 365 nm belong to the n-π* edge leap of the CO bond or CN bond and is the surface absorption state of the 3A-CDs [40]. 3-Aminoquinoline is doped with an N atom containing n electron in the aromatic hydrocarbon system, which is a heterocyclic compound. Compared with benzene ring as raw material, the absorption bands of 3A-CDs are red-shifted and strengthened [41]. As the number of heterocyclic compounds, the absorption wavelength red-shifts more and the absorption intensity increases accordingly [42]. It can be explained that the absorption band of 3A-CDs belongs to the n-π* edge leap of the CO bond or CN bond which extends to 365 nm. The first part of the hydrothermal reaction is the dehydration condensation reaction between –OH, –H, and –COOH between CA molecules [43]. With further polymerization, carbonization and aromatization, sp2 carbon clusters were formed containing a large number of –COOH and –OH groups on the surface [44]. As 3-aminoquinoline began to participate in the reaction, the corresponding absorption peak at 365 nm began to increase, and with the increase of the addition of 3-aminoquinoline, the absorption peak at 365 nm was enhanced. This is because 3-aminoquinoline provides basic conditions for the hydrothermal carbonization process, and the amino group in it will undergo a dehydration condensation reaction with CA and finally form the crosslinked chain connected by an amide group [45]. The N atom exists in two forms in 3A-CDs, one is doped in the aromatic framework of the sp2 carbon clusters in the form of pyridine, pyrrole, and quaternary nitrogen, and the other is attached to the surface of the 3A-CDs by amide bonds. Meanwhile, there is more CO on the surface of the 3A-CDs and the absorption at the 365 nm peak increases.

The abundant functional groups on the 3A-CDs surface cause its absorption and fluorescence efficiency to be considerably affected by the pH value [46]. The 3A-CDs were synthesized according to Table S1, and the results were shown in Fig. 2b. The higher the proportion of 3-aminoquinoline to CA, the better the absorption efficiency of 3A-CDs. The UV absorption intensity of 3A-CDs decreased significantly with increasing the pH value, as shown in Fig. 2c. From this, we can guess that strong acids tend to enhance the UV absorption of 3A-CDs, while alkaline conditions will restrain 3A-CDs. On one hand, it is suggestible to conjugate aromatic π-systems in alkaline conditions, while strong acids enhance the charge transfer of N ions [47]. On the other hand, it could be because of the attribution by the C–N–C bond and –NH2 contained in 3-aminoquinoline. As is known to all, the reaction temperature and reaction time are the most important factors affecting the performance in the hydrothermal carbonization preparation process of CDs. Therefore, three different gradients were set, namely 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C, and 6 h, 8 h, 10 h, and the results are shown in Fig. 2d–e; 180 °C and 8 h are the best conditions, respectively. The concentration of 3A-CDs in aqueous solution was increased, as shown in Fig. 2f. When the concentration of the 3A-CDs aqueous solution was 4.5 μg/mL, the average transmittance of the full UV band was only 0.06%, proving that the absorption performance was excellent. In summary, full UV band absorbing 3A-CDs were successfully synthesized, and the peak at 260 nm rises from the carbon nucleus, including saturated hydrocarbons containing O and N. The CC and CO bonds generated a 320 nm absorption peak, and the peak nearly at 365 nm is from the CN bond.

Due to its size-limiting effect and photoluminescent properties, CDs can emit visible light under UV excitation. The inset of Fig. 3 a shows that 3A-CDs emits bright blue light when exposed to UV light of 365 nm. The 3A-CDs showed an absorption band at 365 nm, which stems from the very uniform size of the luminescence sp2 carbon clusters in the 3A-CDs. The maximum excitation and fluorescence wavelengths were 380 and 460 nm. Meanwhile, the UV absorption curve of 3A-CDs highly coincides with the peak wavelength of the excitation curve, indicating that the 3A-CDs can make full use of the absorbed UV light and convert it into 460 nm visible light [48]. Fig. 3b shows that the fluorescence of 3A-CDs is excitation independent because the surface of the 3A-CDs is completely passivated under the best reaction conditions and raw material ratio; this is only attributed to the single radiation transition of the sp2 carbon cluster. The fluorescence of 3A-CDs is related to its UV absorption, which is greatly affected by pH value, as shown in Fig. 3c–d. When the pH value ranges from 1 to 12, the corresponding fluorescence and excitation wavelengths were first kept at 460 nm and 380 nm, then they took a slight blue-shift to 440 nm and 345 nm, which might be due to the abundance of positively charged surface functional groups.

The aqueous 3A-CDs solution was measured after being irradiated for 0–180 h under a 365 nm UV lamp. It can be found from Fig. 4 a that the color of the aqueous 3A-CDs solution does not change significantly after the aging experiment. The absorption and transmittance curves in Fig. 4b and c shows that the absorption and transmittance of the 3A-CDs are slightly weakened from 0 h to 180 h in the full UV bonds, decreasing only 3.5% and 2.6%, indicating excellent light-aging resistance performance. In order to explore the acid and alkaline resistance of 3A-CDs, FTIR spectra of 3A-CDs with different pH values in solution are shown in Fig. 4d. We can find that the structure of 3A-CDs is not destroyed despite the great change in the pH value of the aqueous solution, only the peaks around 3450 cm−1, 1650 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 slightly increased when the aqueous 3A-CDs solution was acidic, This also confirms the above discussion about the influence of pH value on the UV absorption and luminescence properties of 3A-CDs. Since the environment in which 3A-CDs is applied is commonly not stable, in order to explore the acid and alkaline resistance of UV–Vis blocking efficiency and luminescence wavelength of 3A-CDs in a dynamic environment, the pH value of aqueous 3A-CDs solution underwent three cycles, converting from pH = 3 to pH = 12 and then to pH = 3, repeatedly (Figs. S6–S7). The result is shown in Fig. 4e, a reversible and stable cycle of both UV blocking efficiency and luminescence wavelength is detected. Hydrogen bonds and/or electrostatic forces contribute to the interaction that 3A-CDs produced UV blocking and fluorescence due to the carboxylate protonation - deprotonation theory [49]. In conclusion, 3A-CDs can be confirmed to have excellent stability and are the best choice for outdoor full-UV bond blocking material.

From Fig. 5 a, we can find that the 3A-CDs@PVA film is colourless, with high visible light transmittance, and clear. Rhodamine B was used to account for the UV absorption effect of the 3A-CDs@PVA films which emit bright yellow fluorescence under a UV lamp. Fig. 4b shows that rhodamine B is purple in daylight, and after blocking the UV lamp with a 0 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film, the yellow fluorescence was maintained. However, when we blocked the UV lamp with a 0.3 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film, the yellow fluorescence disappeared, showing the initial purple. It is attested that the PVA film added with 3A-CDs is sufficient to block the UV light. Fig. 4c shows the 3A-CDs@PVA films incorporated with different 3A-CDs contents (0.1 wt% ∼ 0.5 wt%) taken in daylight. The color of the 3A-CDs@PVA films gradually becomes darker but remains highly transparent.

To investigate the UV blocking efficiency of the 3A-CDs@PVA films in real situations, a UV irradiation meter was used to evaluate the blocking efficiency of the 3A-CDs@PVA films, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The 3A-CDs@PVA films were put on the negative carrier and the residual intensity of UV light was measured. In Fig. 6b–c and Figs. S1–S3, the initial UV light intensity from the daylight and UV lamp was 18.940 W/m2 and 67.725 W/m2. As the concentration of doped 3A-CDs increases from 0 to 0.5 wt%, the residual intensity went from 17.182 W/m2 to 0.104 W/m2 and 62.730 W/m2 to 0.210 W/m2, the blocking efficiency increases from 9.28% to 99.45% and 7.37%–99.69%, respectively. The 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA films can nearly block full UV bonds both under daylight and a UV lamp. In addition, as the aging time of 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA increases from 0 to 180 h, the residual intensity went from 0.104 W/m2 to 2.650 W/m2, the blocking efficiency increased from 99.45% to 86.00%, respectively.

The absorption efficiency of the 3A-CDs@PVA films proved (Fig. 7 a) that the pure PVA film has almost no UV absorption intensity, which indicates that the absorption performance of the 3A-CDs@PVA films is almost entirely due to 3A-CDs. Meanwhile, we must truly show the absorption characteristics of the 3A-CDs. We found that the absorption of the 3A-CDs@PVA films with only 0.4 wt% 3A-CDs content could reach 100% in the full UV bonds. With the increasing content of 3A-CDs doped in PVA films, the wavelength of absorption red-shifted, and the 3A-CDs@PVA films changed from simply absorbing UV light to absorbing part of the bonds of blue light, this feature has great application value, such as red-blocking lenses. When the layers of 0.4 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film are superimposed together from one to three in Fig. S1, the blocking bonds are slightly blue-shifted, but the transmittance of visible light is greatly weakened, indicating that the 0.4 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film can achieve a decent effect with only one layer. As shown in Fig. 7b, when the 0.4 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film is exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp uninterruptedly for 180 h, the blocking intensity in the UVA band and the transmittance in the visible light of the 0.4 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film has slightly weakened, confirming that the light aging resistance of the 3A-CDs@PVA films are excellent, and they are suitable for long-term exposure to an outdoor environment. The incorporation of 3A-CDs into the PVA film not only gives the PVA film UV absorption properties but also improves the tensile mechanical properties to a great extent (Fig. 7c). With the increase of the concentration of 3A-CDs, the tensile mechanical properties of the films become stronger, presumably on account of the surface of 3A-CDs having abundant functional groups and cross-linked molecular chains such as –OH, –COOH and –NH2, which can form hydrogen bonds with PVA and alter the orientation of PVA molecular chains, thus improving the mechanical properties significantly. According to the FTIR spectra of 3A-CDs@PVA films doped with different concentrations of 3A-CDs, it can be found that the structure of PVA is not affected by the addition of 3A-CDs, and the peaks at 1350 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 gradually increase with the increase of 3A-CDs concentration. According to the above analysis, these two peaks are the main peaks of 3A-CDs which are due to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, indicating that 3A-CDs are properly doped into PVA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV blocking effect of the new transparent sunscreen umbrella
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the fact that PVA film is susceptible to moisture, it is not ideal to be used as a material for umbrella cloth alone. Considering the fact that most of the sun umbrella materials on the market are opaque and mostly black and not beautiful, 3A-CDs@PVA films were sandwiched into two layers of transparent umbrella cloth. Fig. 8 a shows the UV–Vis transmission spectra of transparent umbrella cloth, 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA films, and 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA films with transparent umbrella cloth. It was found that the transparent umbrella cloth has almost no UV absorption capacity just like the 0 wt% PVA film, and its transmittance in the visible band is similar to 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA films, so it will not influence the transparency of the finished product. The results were as expected, the transmission curve of 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film with umbrella cloth nearly overlaps with the curve of 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film, and the slight reduction in transparency in the visible band is negligible. The 0.5 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film with umbrella cloth was also put on the negative carrier of the UV irradiation meter and the residual intensity of UV was measured. The initial UV intensity in daylight was 18.201 W/m2. As the concentration of doped 3A-CDs increased from 0 to 0.5 wt%, the residual intensity went from 16.082 W/m2 to 0.047 W/m2; the blocking efficiency increased from 7.59% to 99.46%. We calculated the blocking rate of UV, UVA, and UVB, corresponding with SPF and PFA, which is very important for excellent sunscreen products, and visible light transmittance of 3A-CDs@PVA films doped with different concentrations of 3A-CDs with transparent umbrella cloth, as shown in Table 1 .

The UV blocking rates of UV, UVA, UVB and the transmittance (Tvis) of visible light (400−800 nm) were calculated according to eqs (1)−(4) as follows:
(1)
B
U V

Z 0

Z 1

∫ 200 400

d λ

× 100 %
(2)

U V A

∫ 320 400

× 100 %

∫ 280 320

× 100 %
where X(λ) and X0(λ) are the transmittance curves of 3A-CDs@PVA films, respectively, and Z0, Z1, B0, B1, H0, H1, D0, and D1 are the spectral integral areas of X0(λ) and X(λ) in different wavelength intervals [50].

We found that when using SPF as an index to judge the absorption intensity of UVB, the sample could reach an SPF greater than 50 with a concentration of only 0.3 wt%. At the same time, the absorption intensity of UVA is more excellent, all of the 3A-CDs@PVA films with umbrella cloth are PA++++ grade, even the 0.1 wt% one is PA+++. Overall, the application is excellent in terms of both UV blocking capability and stability.

In summary, full UV bond absorption carbon dots (3A-CDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using anhydrous CA and 3-aminoquinoline as raw materials. The 3A-CDs can absorb full UV band and the absorption efficiency, light aging resistance and acid and alkaline resistance was excellent. The absorption and transmittance intensity of the 1.5 μg/mL 3A-CDs aqueous solution was decreased by only 3.5% and 2.6% after exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp for 180 h, the average blocking efficiency is 98.8%. The structure and performance of 3A-CDs were very stable showed in FTIR spectra and acid-alkaline cycle tests. Subsequently, 3A-CDs@PVA films not only with excellent full UV absorption, high visible light transmittance, light aging resistance, but also has stronger mechanical properties than pure PVA. Finally, the 0.3 wt% 3A-CDs@PVA film with transparent umbrella cloth can achieve the blocking efficiency of more than 97.2% in the full UV band, and SPF is greater than 50, PFA is PA++++, and the visible light transmittance is as high as 93.8%, meanwhile, reducing the cost and diluting the color of the film. This study provides a new application and prospects for the development of CDs and their composite products into new, efficient, and durable UV-blocking products and equipment.

Yulin Xu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Visualization, Writing original draft. Zhiqiang Xu: Methodology, Data curation. Congcong Chen: Methodology, Data curation. Weihao Ye: Data curation, Formal analysis. Baoyan Guo: Data curation. Jiemin Qiu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Jianle Zhuang: Data curation. Chaofan Hu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Bingfu Lei: Data curation, Formal analysis. Guangqi Hu: Funding acquisition. Yingliang Liu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
